book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.

lineage: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
Athanasius' Account of the Deeds of Violence committed at Alexandria by George the Arian.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Athanasius' Account of the Deeds of Violence committed at Alexandria by George the Arian.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: What cruelties George perpetrated at Alexandria at the same time may be learned from the narration of Athanasius, who both suffered in and witnessed the occurrences.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: In his ‘Apology for his flight,’ speaking of these transactions, he thus expresses himself:

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: ‘Moreover, they came to Alexandria, again seeking to destroy me: and on this occasion their proceedings were worse than before; for the soldiery having suddenly surrounded the church, there arose the din of war, instead of the voice of prayer.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Afterwards, on his arrival during Lent, George, sent from Cappadocia, added to the evil which he was instructed to work.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: When Easter-week was passed, the virgins were cast into prison, the bishops were led in chains by the military, and the dwellings even of orphans and widows were forcibly entered and their provisions pillaged.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Christians were assassinated by night; houses were sealed; and the relatives of the clergy were endangered on their account.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Even these outrages were dreadful; but those that followed were still more so.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For in the week after the holy Pentecost, the people, having fasted, went forth to the cemetery to pray, because all were averse to communion with George: that wickedest of men being informed of this, instigated against them Sebastian, an officer who was a Manichæan.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He, accordingly, at the head of a body of troops armed with drawn swords, bows, and darts, marched out to attack the people, although it was the Lord’s day: finding but few at prayers,—as the most part had retired because of the lateness of the hour,—he performed such exploits as might be expected from them.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Having kindled a fire, he set the virgins near it, in order to compel them to say that they were of the Arian faith: but seeing they stood their ground and despised the fire, he then stripped them, and so beat them on the face, that for a long time afterwards they could scarcely be recognized.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Seizing also about forty men, he flogged them in an extraordinary manner: for he so lacerated their backs with rods fresh cut from the palm-tree, which still had their thorns on, that some were obliged to resort repeatedly to surgical aid in order to have the thorns extracted from their flesh, and others, unable to bear the agony, died under its infliction.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: All the survivors with one virgin they banished to the Great Oasis.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The bodies of the dead they did not so much as give up to their relatives, but denying them the rites of sepulture they concealed them as they thought fit, that the evidences of their cruelty might not appear.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: They did this acting as madmen.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For while the friends of the deceased rejoiced on account of their confession, but mourned because their bodies were uninterred, the impious inhumanity of these acts was sounded abroad the more conspicuously.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For soon after this they sent into exile out of Egypt and the two Libyas the following bishops: Ammonius, Thmuïs, Caïus, Philo, Hermes, Pliny, Psenosiris, Nilammon, Agatho, Anagamphus, Mark, Ammonius, another Mark, Dracontius, Adelphius, and Athenodorus; and the presbyters Hierax and Discorus.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And so harshly did they treat them in conducting them, that some expired while on their journey, and others in the place of banishment.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: In this way they got rid of more than thirty bishops, for the anxious desire of the Arians, like Ahab’s, was to exterminate the truth if possible.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Such are the words of Athanasius in regard to the atrocities perpetrated by George at Alexandria.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The emperor meanwhile led his army into Illyricum.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For there the urgency of public affairs demanded his presence; and especially the proclamation of Vetranio as emperor by the military.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: On arriving at Sirmium, he came to a conference with Vetranio during a truce; and so managed, that the soldiers who had previously declared for him changed sides, and saluted Constantius alone as Augustus and sovereign autocrat.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: In the acclamations, therefore, no notice was taken of Vetranio.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Vetranio, perceiving himself to be abandoned, immediately threw himself at the feet of the emperor; Constantius, taking from him his imperial crown and purple, treated him with great clemency, and recommended him to pass the rest of his days tranquilly in the condition of a private citizen: observing that a life of repose at his advanced age was far more suitable than a dignity which entailed anxieties and care.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Vetranio’s affairs came to this issue; and the emperor ordered that a liberal provision out of the public revenue should be given him.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Often afterwards writing to the emperor during his residence at Prusa in Bithynia, Vetranio assured him that he had conferred the greatest blessing on him, by liberating him from the disquietudes which are the inseparable concomitants of sovereign power.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Adding that he himself did not act wisely in depriving himself of that happiness in retirement, which he had bestowed upon him.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Let this suffice on this point.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: After these things, the Emperor Constantius having created Gallus his kinsman Cæsar, and given him his own name, sent him to Antioch in Syria, providing thus for the guarding of the eastern parts.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: When Gallus was entering this city, the Savior’s sign appeared in the East: for a pillar in the form of a cross seen in the heavens gave occasion of great amazement to the spectators.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: His other generals the emperor despatched against Magnentius with considerable forces, and he himself remained at Sirmium, awaiting the course of events.
